entry.title: Visual intelligence systems (by andreas blattman) (CS153 S2026)

link.title: Visual intelligence systems (by andreas blattman) (CS153 S2026)

entry.content:
Andreas is a cofounder of frontier AI lab - Black forest labs. Black forest labs has a lot of open models, and also does some consulting, where they provide specialized visual AI models to companies.

Andreas origin story

Andreas did an undergrad in mechanical engineering, and wanted to focus on AI for his PhD. He joined a small lab and studies visual model representation learning in visual modes.

Back in this day, it was all about who could write the paper. Andreas’ lab competed with companies like Google and OpenAI. His lab was quite small so they had to be strategic on what they compete on with big wigs who had much more resources.

His lab focused on finding representations that are equivalent to the pixel space, but much more efficient than tokens (make compute more efficient). This lead to latent generative models, which gave rise to stable diffusion, outdoing competitors.

After this success, his labs next goal was purely focusing on building an image model that is 10 times better than current options. In fact his team spent 3 months just on Flux 1.

Now, he mentions that we need models that learn and interact like us, from natural representations (talking, visual, etc.). He advocates for multimodal models that will outdo everything and it (in theory) can provide a deeper natural understanding of physics as the model can use ANYTHING in the real world as an training example.

The Best Image Model: Creation of Flux

Flux’s training has pre, mid, and post-training.

Pre training

  • has text and images

Mid training

  • adds higher resolution and more capabilities

Post training

  • includes offline distillation to be more efficient and match customer needs before deployment

From systems perspective: Feedback loop is clsoed when the tech is used to solve real problems, providing data on what users care about. Ex. Flux improved character consistency within 60 days, when they released Flux 1 Context, a special editing model.

Next wave of models

Andreas says the purpose of shifting toward multimodal models is to move beyond content creation towards physical AI, robotics, and world simulation.

Historically, it has been difficult to get models to understand semantics. Ex. when using a representation learning pretrained model, they were restricted with trying to match the transformer backbone to another model. This technique was limited to a single modality (unimodal), such as just images Because it relied on these specific pre-trained “anchors,” the system was restricted to that specific domain and could not easily scale to understand how different types of data (like audio and video) relate to each other

Self Flow was discussed as a way of solving multimodal reasoning.

Self Flow

While previous alignment techniques were restricted to a single modality, Self Flow allows a model to align its internal representations across multiple modalities.

This is done during pre-training to build a general representation of the world before adding specific tasks like “action prediction” (e.g., predicting a keystroke or robot movement) during mid-training.

Andreas reccomends this data labeling strategy:

  • Pretraining (quantity): use lots of noisy data. Labels handled automatically
  • Post-training (quality): shift to high quality data and human signals.

Iterative denoising and model differences

Both diffusion and language models are iterative but:

Diffusion: iterative in an artificial time dimension that moves from noise to image.

  • This is data inefficient - each example generate infinite potential losses across noise to data trajectory (you can distil from any time point)
    • But we can distil diffusion models better than language
    • BFL use latent adversarial distillation to reduce steps to generate image from 50 to 4 steps (we generate image extremely fast)

Language: iterative in direction of data, generating one token at a time. Tokens can be parallized so its data efficient - Con: Inference takes lots of time

(Future) How do we build spatial intelligence in 3D world

Andreas believes that all a model needs to understand space is the ability to observe natural representations (video) and interact with objects. 3D representations are notoriously difficult - Existing 3D representations are narrow, inflexible, and static (point clouds) - while a model might develop an implicit 3D structure within its neural weights to understand the world, the interface for learning and interaction should remain natural (video/audio) rather than being forced through explicit 3D priors

Overall thoughts

It is a common sentiment, but its clear Andreas agrees with the idea that a multimodal model should have a single transformer backbone rather than hack a bunch of good LLMs, VLMs, together.

On the 3D debate, Andreas mentions the importance of focusing on working with EXISTING training data over trying to make your own data sets. (this point interests me: i feel like there many companies focused on making datasets, which are doing quite well.) I guess his point his to make a foundation model it needs to be able generalize. Then at that point specialized datasets become valuable. Cool.

the trend with andreas: he says with frontier AI you optomize inference via architectural distillation, sicne image models used to be really data inefficeitn and slow at inference times.
entry.summary: Andreas talks about visual systems, and his experience from his tiny lab to how his startup is doing.

entry.title: Product management in the AI era (n. singhal from skip) (CS153 S2026)

link.title: Product management in the AI era (n. singhal from skip) (CS153 S2026)

entry.content:
what is PM?

  • people who glue creative ideas to the engineers

PM has different jobs at different stages of a startup

  1. product market fit

Here, founders experiment rapidly to find what sticks with customers.

  1. post product market fit

Here, experimentation stops. Stop commuincation between teams and focus 100% on that product. You MUST meet customer expectations.

  1. hypergrowth

PM manages the organizations’ scale, and has 2 goals:

  • scale product to more users
  • expand to adjacent product lines
  1. late stage

PMS fight innovators dilemma, by creating zero to one products with large corporate structure

the shift from info mover to product builder

Information movement is handled by AI agents (the forward deployed engineer is dying as a result).

  • Middle managers will also die since they can’t reinvent themselves.

the modern PM’s value lies in judgement, decision-making, and having a strong opinion on what should be built and why.

  • He argues this role is a lot more interesting

Merging roles (PMs, designers, and engineers)

  • Modern product people are expected to be gritty and technical, often using AI tools to build and validate their ideas directly rather than just managing others who build them

  • it becomes less about people management and more about system design. you need to decide what to build

career advice

Your tech career is 40-50 years, averaging 15-18 jobs YOUR GOAL: each job should build on the other. SO, ensure first role maximizes opportunities for the second

Now PMs are expected to be technical:

  • You should leave a company to be part of one that grows faster than you
  • You shouldn’t be the best in your company

how to get a job as a college student?

Be modern

  • Use latest tools (AI agents, claud code)
  • Have opinion on what NEEDS to be BUILT
  • IMPLEMENT stuff. Be hands on over learning theory

Learn beyond programming

  • keep a systems mindset ( As knowledge becomes a service and AI handles more of the construction, the primary problem becomes: “Does it fit, should you build it, and how do you know if it’s working?” )

This skill involves:

  • Understanding how different stacks work and evolve
  • Evaluating if a project aligns with a company’s brand or system design
  • Thinking about where a product fits into the broader technological landscape

Succeed in unstructured places

  • thrive without structure and feedback (there are less managers)
  • Maintain an Undying Urgency to ship and experiment

building a talent agency

Skip.show: A website featuring content and a Substack focused on product leadership Skip Coach: help tech professionals stay current regarding how AI is changing company building - Practitioners vs. Learners: He distinguishes between people “practicing the craft” and those “learning the craft.” He argues that once a community scales and prioritizes monetization, it tends to attract learners, causing elite operators at the top of their game to leave - He only keeps 120 people in this group, because they have limited time - AI will kill off the ‘career development’ agency led by humans

random advice

Value customer needs over what is ‘most valuable’ to your perspective. For example nobody wanted 1 app to do all messaging (Google hangouts). Whatsapp purely focused on text messages.

It doesn’t matter how you start but how fast you improve it (speed of iteration)

Why large companies fail?

  • they are symptoms of scale, and culture.
    1. they constantly place massive bets on products to keep growing infinitely
    2. sunk cost fallacy
    3. culture dictates product strategy. Google: highly reactive, iterative fast and pull back fast if something fails Meta/Apple: founder driven. you can’t achieve greatness by agreement within a company. Zuck forced himself into metaverse, but it wasn’t the worst thing ever
entry.summary: We talk about the future of the role of product management, as they become so much more productive with AI

entry.title: Unified intelligence systems - Amit jain luma (CS153 S2026)

link.title: Unified intelligence systems - Amit jain luma (CS153 S2026)

entry.content:
Cofounder of Luma, Amit Jain, gives his lecture. we talk in detail about the idea and necessity of a unified model.

Origin story

Amit saw that if you combined language model scaling with differentiable 3D learning, you could create a training loop capable of optimizng EVERY observation in the universe.

The goal is move away from traditional 3D reconstruction and use generative models to make 3D representations (like point clouds and meshes).

Amit’s point: if a system can learn about the universe, it can generate it.

Luma’s beginning by capturing 3D Data

Luma released the Luma 3D Capture app, which quickly became popular by being the first to productionize NeRF (Neural Radiance Fields) and Gaussian Splats for everyday users.

But it can’t keep up with exisiting data growth

Pivot to generative video

Following Nvidia’s Hopper architecture, Luma shifts to video generation.

Amit sees video as a 3D medium (2 spatial dimensions and 1 time dimension).

Released Dream Machine, a video gen model.

how to achieve unified intelligence

By 2025, we realized pure video models are just pixel generators.

To create more understanding, we need UNIFIED intelligence.

In the future we use a single transformer backbone where all multimodal data are encoded in the same space.

differentiable world learning

for deep learning to exist, a funciton must be differentiable so it works with gradient descent

  • transformers use gradient descent really well

the luma factory

also humans are very picky in what they like

  • Luma monitors what users likes, CONSTANTLY, and uses this to update their model

The pipelien is also standard: pre, mid, and post training.

  • it is trained on visual and audio simulataneously, on 30 petabytes.

what we do differently:

  • instead of scaling parameters (other companies), we operate on subscale compute and data infra
  • they dont focus on heavy domains like code.

the understanding and generation chasm

  • VLM can understand image, but not generate
  • image gen models can create visuals but can’t reason about it
  • LLMs cant understand visual

the answer is clear, we need to stuff all modalities on transformer backbone.

BUT is extremely capital intensive.

  • Whoever pays the massive upfront premium to build a unified physical world model will completely bypass text-only systems and own the foundational tech for both media creation and robotics

random advice

People do their best work when they are unconstrained - Real creators produce so much work and you can’t measure the value of it - If you measure everything you produce, you’ll never be a creator

if your arch can’t scale with compute + data it dies

thats why theres a graveyard of visual architectures (GANs)

- GANs are dead because they are rigid: GANs are like two networks aggressively fighting each other. They rely on old convolution-based layers that break when you try to make them bigger. They don't scale.  

- Diffusion models are hitting a wall: Even though diffusion powers today's popular image models, Amit claims its physics don't scale well. Diffusion has "bad habits" when generating long video or complex timelines, meaning pure diffusion is also on its way out

THE ONLY REPLACEMENT for issue is a UNIFIED MODEL.

random, but whats a possible industry for unified world models?

Hollywood is dead. They act like private equity firms—spending $500 million to produce 5 sequels because they are scared of new ideas.

Netflix won by taking smaller budgets and launching 800 productions a year to see what sticks.

Amit: Physical movie production is insanely expensive and slow. Luma’s unified world model allows a director to test 500 different physical scenes, explosions, or alternative story timelines unconstrained by costs.
entry.summary: Andreas talks about visual systems, and his experience from his tiny lab to how his startup is doing.

entry.title: CS153 Frontier Systems Summer 2026 Lecture 1 notes

link.title: CS153 Frontier Systems Summer 2026 Lecture 1 notes

entry.content:
I got into looking at these lectures by looking at the lineup of speakers they had. It was completely stacked with big names from industry (Sam Altman, Ben Horowitz, Jensen, etc.). But the comment section says its just ‘AI Coachella’, meaning nothing of substance is being said, and its mainly for maintaining the interviewee’s image.

I decided to take a look at it myself, is there really of anything of substance said?

The first lecture is:

Lets takea field-trip into the 2026 Frontier AI factory

In 2026 the way we make infrastructure for AI is changing.

The AI scaling loop is get compute -> get data -> model results, put the money back into compute. Bottlenecks include how much compute you have, the public’s perception, and how much capital you have Most companies (like in YC) follow these three steps:

  1. incubation (focus on what part of the system you want to attack)
  2. SOTA release
  3. expand model to more capabilities / diversify offerings

Its fun to take apart new companies and see how many are actually following this roadmap.

THE ultimate scaling law:

  • More compute turns into better models, which turns into better revenue From a systems perspective, you can convert between infra and compute: $1 of infrastructure turns into $1 of revenue. GUARANTEED. WOW what a claim.

Think about it. Literally every big company is exponentially investing in infrastructure. For a reason right? Example: The H100 GPU is a 2 year old chip, but the cost to use them is peaking NOW.

  • Computing chips is not just a commodity that depreciates over time

examples of products that followed this law:

Steel: Hoarded until industrialization caused prices to crash. Fiber Optics: Over-expansion led to internet transit prices dropping 90% in 5 years. DRAM: Panic/world events trigger infrastructure sell-offs before the market stabilizes. Uranium: Value peaked, crashed after major incidents, and was ultimately stabilized by government intervention. Stabilization Timeline: Historically takes 3 years for digital commodities and 6 years for physical infrastructure.

Compute is Not Yet a Commodity

Unlike traditional commodities, compute is currently non-fungible and incredibly difficult to forecast. This means chips are not interchangeable; an H100 GPU cannot be swapped for a H50.

Path to Standardization of Compute

To turn compute into a true, tradeable commodity, the industry requires trusted standards and institutions to establish fungibility.

4 Requirements for Compute Fungibility:

  1. Common Unit: A universal metric of measurement.
  2. Standard Delivery Interface: Uniform access points.
  3. Interconnection / Pooling: The ability to combine resources.
  4. Commercial Infrastructure: Reliable metering, control, and settlement.

Current State: We are currently in the pre-standardization era of compute—akin to the internet before the adoption of the HTTPS standard.

extra thoughts

Anjey seems seriously happy go lucky and optimistic about how AI will 100% without a doubt scale. Though his points are not nearly as thorough as Ronald Coases’ theories, it is likely just a result of the times we live in. Attention spans are going down so seriously anything with a moderate amount of value gets popular.

Never mind -> its not just attention spans are going down. What is throwing me off is that instead of providing examples, he should explain in more depth what it means that infra can directly convert to revenue.

on the positive side -> he makes the concrete claim that AI is in the prestandardization era. which means AI will get standardized too, and turned into something fungible. and that AI infra directly translates to revenue.
entry.summary: i watched all the lectures from CS153 but totally forgot everything. ill consolidate my notes here.

entry.title: ElevenLabs Mati Staniszewski on Audio AI (CS153 S2026)

link.title: ElevenLabs Mati Staniszewski on Audio AI (CS153 S2026)

entry.content:
The first guest speaker is Mati Staniszewski, Cofounder of ElevenLabs. yes that same elevenlabs that seems to be at every hackathon in the GTA. it also got moderately viral, for its AI voice stuff.

ive tried it out before, its really optimized. like all you do is send an api request and you can get audio output seriously fast. although the company is really diversified (applications are endless with AI text / audio models), its not really focused one one thing. but it kinda makes sense, I think ElevenLabs is trying to get the a fat market share before finding their niche…

lets see Mati’s mindset

Mati origin story

Did PhD and worked at some big company and found a co founder somewhere (very generic)

as a polish kid:

  • wanted to watch well acted dubbed movies

the next step? it is to figure out

  1. research potential
  2. demand

after talking to people:

  • dubbing is complicated (need 3 models patched together. sound to text, translate, then text to sound)
  • creators want to edit their own audio too instead of just dubbing We decided to zero in on focusing on text to speech

our innovations:

  • prompt LLM to take context, and decide its own voice characteristics
  • took ideas from academic research and kept prototyping

what is elevenlabs

purpose:

  • build products and research AI foundation models for voice systems

how they got popular?

  • tried to involve the community at every step. got popular by testing products via Discord bots, for free.
  • they stayed problem-obsessed, focused on SOTA voice systems

elevenlabs roadmap

2022 - research how to bring context from text 2023 - test text to speech narration for book authors 2024-25 - zero in on translating speeches in real time

2026 - building a SOTA approach

we have 2 approaches: Fused approach

  • 1 model handles all tasks (to text, translate, etc.)
  • it is faster but not reliable or trackable

Cascaded approach

  • patch multiple LLMs together
  • it is more reliable, and we can keep track of the model

2027+ emotionality and the reliability-latency trade-off

on emotionality

  • we believe tech will automatically fix the computers ability to express emotions with context
  • but it is still important to consider - how can you make interaction a truly real experience

on reliabilty-latency tradeoff - lets combine the fused and cascaded approach

Lets use the example of customer service rep:

  • use fused approach for the initial, informal part of a conversation (ie. the small talk, where feel is important)
  • the moment the convo needs a reliable action (payment), we swap to a cascaded approach to ensure the agent is correct

what else is next

the great shift to platform value

As model improvements plateau, the value will shift to the platform itself This involves:

  • Deep Problem Solving: Providing creators and developers with tools to solve specific problems.
  • Business Integration: Becoming the infra for business-to-audience conversation
  • Market Positioning: Mati envisions a future where three to five major platforms serve the world’s conversational needs, similar to how a few major companies dominate cloud computing

The ‘next big thing’ is not a platform - its blurring the line between tools and platforms

  • in the future, the value is not in research, but in building the pipes around it. Think about valuable knowledge bases, communication pipelines, and constantly evaluating the AI

random

  • China has attempted distillation attacks (trying to extract the intelligence of a model to replicate it) on Western companies like ElevenLabs

extra thoughts

Frontier systems can innovate without massive compute at the start. All you need is to obsess over a problem

Not all ai is end to end (think instead of prompt -> result, the user has more control on the AI)

  • stop being limited in what AI can do, and try to have it actually assist the human. for example customer service routing, instead of handling all of the customers

anjey trying to relate this back to systems somehow

In the systems world there are 2 types of people (they both have a collaborative point of view)

  • You want to be one of the hyper scalers or legacy companies

Elevan labs has dmall teams, each have discretion to take action

  • Revenue scales with deployment (systems level perspective)

Its still hard to predict if we get money though

  • But if you are surrounded by capable people - you can predict they will be successful
  • Always work backwards from the value you deliver to the customer. Never from the cost it takes to run
  • You want to profit 1/10th of what you deliver
entry.summary: Mati describes the state of audio AI in 2026, his pathway, and what the company is doing in the future.

entry.title: exploring ‘the bitter lesson’ by richard sutton (2019)

link.title: exploring ‘the bitter lesson’ by richard sutton (2019)

entry.content:
Looking to improve methods with just human knowledge is a waste of time

  • AI researchers try to build knowledge into their agents, which helps in short term
  • But plateaus in long term and inhibits progress

Eventually a breakthrough is achieved which scales computation by search and learning

  • General purpose methods are powerful (methods that scale with increased computation even as available computation becomes great)
  • The two methods that seem to scale in this way are search and learning

Overall We should invest in meta methods that allow computer to capture the arbitrary complexity, to find good approximations. We want AI to discover like we can, not contain what we discovered

My thoughts

Cool piece by richie here. I like the way he writes, and how he decomposed the original scaling laws into simply “more compute = better”. what he is saying is that its just about hardware - literally ANYTHING that can use (leverage) more compute is a good strategy.

So its a good thing to keep in mind that having some type of human experience that you resonate with might not work with AI. taking the chess example from richie here, people’s human intuition in building chess bots failed in comparison to a simple search based prorgram that ran on specialized hardware. People said if they had that much compute their theory would be better, but it did not do better.

The main point is to SCALE COMPUTE. just run a bigger search algorithm , and allow more learning to happen.

From this paper I get the path AGI happens when we make AI discover relentlessly.
entry.summary: just saw a tweet saying i should read this, and it was a little dense in writing. so i rewrote the main points here.

entry.title: my notes on the nature of the firm (ronald coase 1937)

link.title: my notes on the nature of the firm (ronald coase 1937)

entry.content:
before we start - who wrote ‘the nature of the firm’?

Ronald Coase wrote this research paper published in Economica 1937, which won a nobel in 1991. He got inspiration for this paper when visited the US on a scholarship at the age of 21.

He spent the next year interviewing business leaders and touring facilities. Many of the people he spoke to would go on and on about the idea of an ‘invisible hand’. The invisible hand refers to the fact that the free market perfectly coordinates supply and demand and rather than explain what the hand is, people believed in this ‘hand’.

Coase did not like this explanation, rather you get the vibe that he knows there ought to be an actual reason, and the choice to overlook this makes traditional economists lazy. His counter is saying that if the market automatically allocates resources, then there is no reason for a company to exist.

Anywho now that you know his beef with lazy economists treating firms and the economy as a black box, the ‘human condition’, and the motivation to be thorough, we can start his paper.

Introduction

When studying the economy, it is EXTREMELY important to state your assumptions so decision-making is better. Coase describes why it is important, but I did not find anything significant to mention here.

ANY assumption in economics must be TRACTABLE and REALISTIC. but more often than not:

  • if a theory is realistic: it is not trackable
  • if a theory is tractable with math, then it relies on unrealistic assumption

Coases’ goal is create a definition for a firm that is both realistic and trackable.

I - in search of a definition of a firm

What do we currently believe?

  • We believe the economy works itself. It has no central control
  • The invisible hand automatically adjusts demand and consumption into a balance

What assumption does this theory make?

  • That the direction of resources (like any transaction) is completely dependent on price

BUT - this is not observed in the real world

  • Say we have the price of product A is higher in company X than in Y
  • So consumers would move from X to Y.
  • But products X and Y also represent departments within a firm. Here a boss can order someone to work on X. So he works on X, not because of price but because he’s being ordered to.

Analogy - think of the economy as ice cubes in a bucket of water

  • in the water (economy), the production is directed by complex system of price movements and exchanges
  • Inside the ice (firm), complex structure is replaced by strict entrepreneur-worker relationship who directs production. Here we have centralized planning. Coase says the firm ‘supersedes’ the complex open market with its own strict centralized control.

II - why firms emerge?

Firstly - What if firms exist just because people have an emotional preference for working in a hierarchy instead of on the open market?

  • It can’t be because of emotional reasons. Like some people like being bosses and some like being workers. but it does not tractably describes the firms existence

Lets first define the price mechanism

  • it refers the cost of using the open market, which we call transaction cost.
  • these transaction costs include costs accrued from searching, negotiating, and actually making exchange transactions.

firms deal with less transaction costs

  • since firms deal with less transaction costs -> they dont rely on price mechanism

example Imagine someone places long term order for product. But it is difficult to predict demand.

  • The longer the contract, the less possible it is for the customer to specify what the company is expected to do. So these details are specified later. You can’t get this on the open market.

Firms specifically emerge because the future is too unpredictable to rely on individual market contracts.

the opposing theory of why firms exist (professor knight’s theory)

Knight says firms exist because of uncertainty and people’s adverse reaction to risk. Argues that the paycheck structure (getting a guaranteed, fixed wage) is the entire reason a firm exists.

  • Coase: this is irrelevant. Aan independent freelancer on the open market can sign a contract for a fixed, guaranteed payment. You don’t need a boss for a steady paycheck.

Coase moves back to his question: Why do we stop using the open market and start letting bosses tell us what to do? (And his answer, as always, is transaction costs).

Another factor of why trandsaction costs are lower in firms?

  • there is less transaction cost WITHIN a firm. These encourage firms growth over the open market, but its not a reason for why firms exist

Ok. but Coase - can you tell us scientifically why its like that?

Knights’ definition of when a firm gets larger or smaller:

  • Firms size is determined by ‘personality and historical accident’ over actual principles
  • Firms don’t get infinitely big : there is an opposing force that makes them less efficient as they get bigger.

Coase: Knights’ theory is lazy and GARBAGE.

Coases’ on why the world isn’t one giant firm?

  • As firm gets larger, organising additional transactions may increase cost, up until it is = open market (diminishing returns)
  • As number of transactions increase, it is harder for entrepreneurs to make efficient (diminishing returns). Again increase until price = market

He describes this as: Firms limit because of diminishing returns to management and the rising costs of organizing internal transactions.

BUT, there is a paradox:

If 1 firm can’t get too big, then two firms can handle different parts of the supply chain still below market prices?

The Setup: Firm A and Firm B trade on the market because both currently operate below their maximum efficiency limits.

The Reality: Firm B handles multiple production stages; if Firm A wants to eliminate market fees entirely, it cannot just absorb one task—it must swallow Firm B’s entire complex operation.

The Decision: Firm A will only merge if the massive internal cost of managing all of B’s operations is cheaper than just paying the open market transaction fee

our major assumption:

  • we assumed transactions are homogenous.
  • note that transaction costs vary heavily: so the open market with ALWAYS exist alongside firms
  • BUT, transaction diversity does not explain why we have many firms

so how do firms grow?

  • Efficient Management (Lower Bureaucracy Costs): A firm will grow larger if it is simply cheaper to organize tasks internally
  • Better Decision Making: A firm can get much bigger if the entrepreneur is highly skilled at avoiding errors. [so improving management techniques = bigger firm]. also, bringing factors of produciton closer (lessen the chaotic distribution), helps firm grow (think of the invention of phone).
  • Scale advantage: firm get better deals on resources

we have 2 ways a firm can expand:

  1. Combination is when a transaction organized by 2+ entrepreneurs is now organized by 1
  2. This is integration when it involves organizing transactions previously carried out between entrepreneurs

III - disproving other explanations of why firms emerge

Usher and Dobb argue

  • Firms exist because of complexity of dividing labour. Firms act as the integrating force that keeps labour together

Coase calls them stupido:

  • He says the economy already has an integrating force - the ‘open market’ coordinates labour automatically
  • Economists already agree that as long as open market exists, division of labor does not lead to chaos

Professor Knight argues

  • In a perfect world where everyone knows the future, the economy would run without firms
  • But real life has a lot of uncertainty which splits people into two groups: the confident and venturesome (Entrepreneurs), and doubtful and timid (regular workers)

Brave people take financial risk and guarantee wages to workers in exchange for power

Coase: this theory does not explain why people choose firms over open market

  1. You don’t need to be a firm to be brave and make good decisions (consulting)
  2. Control is still taken even without guaranteed payments (consulting)

IV - relating this to ‘cost curve of the firm’

Defining the cost curve = graph that shows how much it costs a company to produce goods as it gets bigger. in theory people use the cost curve to predict how big a firm will get

Mainstream theory

  • Firm expands until the cost to make one extra item equal money they get from selling it (marginal revenue). Coase’s:
  • This theory assumed a company is only allowed to make 1 product
  • If product B’s cost curve gets too high, the firm expands to products A and C. it doesn’t stop growing

So the cost curve is not a good indicator of when a company will stop growing if it plans to make to products.

is our firm definition realistic?

Firstly: Employees vs independent contractors

  1. Contractor (the market): you buy the fruits of their labour, do not tell how to do their job
  2. Employee (firm): the boss has legal right to control the servant The difference is not how they get paid (fixed vs variable), but the freedom they have to approach the work The law says defining characteristics of employment relationship is control

Coase reaches same conclusion

  • Firm exists the moment when open market’s price is superseded by an entrepreneur’s direct command
  • This lines up perfectly with real-world definition

Is my definition manageable?

  • Yes. you can plug my theory in with math to calculate when a firm should stop growing

Business owners constantly experiment to find balance between internal and market costs - It is a moving equilibrium

  • business owners must always forecast the future by making contracts, and manage the existing team within the firm to always be efficient
entry.summary: the nature of the firm is really popular but was too hard for me to read. here is a digestible version with some of my thoughts
